infobox.value: ([Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"): ตาก, pronounced [[tàːk]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Thai "Help:IPA/Thai")) is one of Thailand's seventy-seven [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Thailand "Provinces of Thailand") (*changwat*) and lies in [Western Thailand](/wiki/Western_Thailand "Western Thailand"). Neighbouring provinces are (from north clockwise) [Mae Hong Son](/wiki/Mae_Hong_Son_province "Mae Hong Son province"), [Chiang Mai](/wiki/Chiang_Mai_province "Chiang Mai province"), [Lamphun](/wiki/Lamphun_province "Lamphun province"), [Lampang](/wiki/Lampang_province "Lampang province"), [Sukhothai](/wiki/Sukhothai_province "Sukhothai province"), [Kamphaeng Phet](/wiki/Kamphaeng_Phet_province "Kamphaeng Phet province"), [Nakhon Sawan](/wiki/Nakhon_Sawan_province "Nakhon Sawan province"), [Uthai Thani](/wiki/Uthai_Thani_province "Uthai Thani province") and [Kanchanaburi](/wiki/Kanchanaburi_province "Kanchanaburi province"). The western edge of the province has a long boundary with [Kayin State](/wiki/Kayin_State "Kayin State") of Myanmar ([Burma](/wiki/Burma "Burma")).

**Tak** ([Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language"): ตาก, pronounced [[tàːk]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Thai "Help:IPA/Thai")) is one of Thailand's seventy-seven [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Thailand "Provinces of Thailand") (*changwat*) and lies in [Western Thailand](/wiki/Western_Thailand "Western Thailand"). Neighbouring provinces are (from north clockwise) [Mae Hong Son](/wiki/Mae_Hong_Son_province "Mae Hong Son province"), [Chiang Mai](/wiki/Chiang_Mai_province "Chiang Mai province"), [Lamphun](/wiki/Lamphun_province "Lamphun province"), [Lampang](/wiki/Lampang_province "Lampang province"), [Sukhothai](/wiki/Sukhothai_province "Sukhothai province"), [Kamphaeng Phet](/wiki/Kamphaeng_Phet_province "Kamphaeng Phet province"), [Nakhon Sawan](/wiki/Nakhon_Sawan_province "Nakhon Sawan province"), [Uthai Thani](/wiki/Uthai_Thani_province "Uthai Thani province") and [Kanchanaburi](/wiki/Kanchanaburi_province "Kanchanaburi province"). The western edge of the province has a long boundary with [Kayin State](/wiki/Kayin_State "Kayin State") of Myanmar ([Burma](/wiki/Burma "Burma")).

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tak_province&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Geography")]

The [Bhumibol Dam](/wiki/Bhumibol_Dam "Bhumibol Dam") (named after [King Bhumibol Adulyadej](/wiki/King_Bhumibol_Adulyadej "King Bhumibol Adulyadej"), the old name was *Yanhee Dam*) is in Khao Kaew [Tambon](/wiki/Tambon "Tambon") (sub-district), [Sam Ngao District](/wiki/Sam_Ngao_District "Sam Ngao District") of Tak and was built from 1958 to 1964.[[5]](#cite_note-RidTh-5) It stops the [river Ping](/wiki/Ping_River "Ping River"), one of the two sources of the [Chao Phraya](/wiki/Chao_Phraya "Chao Phraya") River.[[6]](#cite_note-RID_River_Gauges-6)[[7]](#cite_note-WorldWater-7)[[8]](#cite_note-WorldWaterDetail-8) The artificial lake created covers an area of 300 km2 and is the largest in Thailand. [Taksin Maharat National Park](/wiki/Taksin_Maharat_National_Park "Taksin Maharat National Park"), [Namtok Pha Charoen National Park](/wiki/Namtok_Pha_Charoen_National_Park "Namtok Pha Charoen National Park"), [Lan Sang National Park](/wiki/Lan_Sang_National_Park "Lan Sang National Park"), and [Khun Phawo National Parks](/wiki/Khun_Phawo_National_Park "Khun Phawo National Park") are all in the province. [Thungyai Naresuan Wildlife Sanctuary](/wiki/Thungyai_Naresuan_Wildlife_Sanctuary "Thungyai Naresuan Wildlife Sanctuary") shares half of the lake front with Kanchanaburi and [Huai Kha Khaeng Wildlife Sanctuary](/wiki/Huai_Kha_Khaeng_Wildlife_Sanctuary "Huai Kha Khaeng Wildlife Sanctuary") at the border with Uthai Thani and are [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site").[[9]](#cite_note-UNEPT-9)[[10]](#cite_note-UNEP-10)

On the western side of Tak province the [Tenasserim Hills](/wiki/Tenasserim_Hills "Tenasserim Hills") meet the [Dawna Range](/wiki/Dawna_Range "Dawna Range"). One of the few transnational roads and cross-border points into [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar") is at [Mae Sot](/wiki/Mae_Sot "Mae Sot"). Northwest of Mae Sot the main road on the Thai side skirts the border until it turns straight north towards [Mae Hong Son](/wiki/Mae_Hong_Son "Mae Hong Son").

Tak province occupies 17,303 km2 (6,681 sq mi) and lies 426 km north of Bangkok. The total forest area is 12,455 km2 (4,809 sq mi) or 72 percent of provincial area.[[1]](#cite_note-AREA-1)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tak_province&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: History")]

Tak was a historical kingdom built over 2,000 years ago, even before the [Sukhothai period](/wiki/Sukhothai_kingdom "Sukhothai kingdom"). The ancient kingdom had its peak around the 1st century. By the 5th century the capital of this kingdom was moved south to Lavo (present day [Lopburi province](/wiki/Lopburi_province "Lopburi province")). A city named [Ban Tak](/wiki/Amphoe_Ban_Tak "Amphoe Ban Tak") was established by [Jamadevi](/wiki/Jamadevi "Jamadevi") (พระนางจามเทวี), princess of the Lavo kingdom, around 663 CE. It became part of the [Sukhothai kingdom](/wiki/Sukhothai_kingdom "Sukhothai kingdom") through battles led by [Ramkhamhaeng the Great](/wiki/Ramkhamhaeng_the_Great "Ramkhamhaeng the Great") and formed the main fortress on the western front. The city was moved further west and renamed *Mueang Rahang* when the [Ayuthaya kingdom](/wiki/Ayuthaya_kingdom "Ayuthaya kingdom") was lost to [Burma](/wiki/Burma "Burma") during [King Maha Thammaracha's](/wiki/Mahathammarachathirat_(king_of_Ayutthaya) "Mahathammarachathirat (king of Ayutthaya)") reign. The city was moved back to the east side of the [Ping River](/wiki/Ping_River "Ping River") during the early [Bangkok period](/wiki/Bangkok_period "Bangkok period").[[14]](#cite_note-Jamadevi-14)

King [Taksin](/wiki/Taksin "Taksin") was vice-governor of Tak before the Ayutthaya kingdom fell during the war with Burma. As his name was *Sin*, he became called *Tak-Sin* during his time in Tak.[[15]](#cite_note-Taksin-15)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tak_province&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Demographics")]

About a quarter of the population belongs to one of Thailand's tribe [hill tribes](/wiki/Hill_tribe_(Thailand) "Hill tribe (Thailand)"): [Yao](/wiki/Yao_people_(Asia) "Yao people (Asia)"), [Karen](/wiki/Karen_people "Karen people") ([Thai](/wiki/Thai_language "Thai language") *Kariang*), [Akha](/wiki/Akha_language "Akha language") (Thai *Akha*), [Lahu](/wiki/Lahu_people "Lahu people") (Thai *Musoe*), [Hmong](/wiki/Hmong_people "Hmong people") (Thai *Mong*), and [Lisu](/wiki/Lisu_people "Lisu people") (Thai *Lisaw*).[[16]](#cite_note-Tribes-16) The largest tribe in Tak is Karen.[[17]](#cite_note-Karen-17)

## Refugees

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tak_province&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Refugees")]

According to the [UNHCR](/wiki/United_Nations_High_Commissioner_for_Refugees "United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees") data of 2008, nearly 95,000 of Thailand's 121,000 registered [refugees](/wiki/Refugee "Refugee") from [Burma](/wiki/Burma "Burma") are housed in several refugee camps in Tak province of which [Mae La camp](/wiki/Mae_La_refugee_camp "Mae La refugee camp") is the largest with around 45,000 [Karen](/wiki/Karen_people "Karen people") refugees.[[18]](#cite_note-UNrefugee-18)

## Symbols

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tak_province&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Symbols")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Bhumibol_dam_front.jpg/250px-Bhumibol_dam_front.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bhumibol_dam_front.jpg)

Bhumibol Dam

The [provincial seal](/wiki/Seals_of_the_provinces_of_Thailand "Seals of the provinces of Thailand") shows King [Naresuan](/wiki/Naresuan "Naresuan") on the royal elephant.[[19]](#cite_note-Tak_province-19) Sometimes below the elephant a [garuda](/wiki/Garuda "Garuda") is depicted, as the garuda is the state symbol of Thailand. King Naresuan is shown pouring consecrated water on the ground, a symbolic act to declare independence. This refers to the war of 1584 with [Burma](/wiki/Burma "Burma"), when Tak was the first border town to be liberated from Burmese control.

The provincial slogan is, "A town of wonderful nature, huge Bhumiphol Dam, King Taksin The Great and beautiful forests".[[19]](#cite_note-Tak_province-19)

The [provincial tree](/wiki/List_of_Thai_provincial_trees "List of Thai provincial trees") is the [Asian Jatoba](/w/index.php?title=Asian_Jatoba&action=edit&redlink=1 "Asian Jatoba (page does not exist)") (*[Xylia xylocarpa](/wiki/Xylia_xylocarpa "Xylia xylocarpa")* var. *kerrii*), and the provincial flower is the Orchid tree ([*Bauhinia* sp.](/wiki/Bauhinia "Bauhinia")). The cyprinid fish *[Hypsibarbus malcolmi](/wiki/Hypsibarbus_malcolmi "Hypsibarbus malcolmi")* is the provincial aquatic life.

## Health

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tak_province&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Health")]

There are two main public hospitals in Tak: [Somdejphrajaotaksin Maharaj Hospital](/wiki/Somdejphrajaotaksin_Maharaj_Hospital "Somdejphrajaotaksin Maharaj Hospital") and Mae Sot Hospital, both operated by the [Ministry of Public Health](/wiki/Ministry_of_Public_Health_(Thailand) "Ministry of Public Health (Thailand)").

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tak_province&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Economy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Tak_province_Burmese_mountains.jpg/250px-Tak_province_Burmese_mountains.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tak_province_Burmese_mountains.jpg)

Cornfields and the mountains of Burma south of Mae Sot

Agriculture is a major part of the Tak economy. The province of Tak produces [rice](/wiki/Rice "Rice"), [corn](/wiki/Maize "Maize"), [vegetables](/wiki/Vegetable "Vegetable"), [fruits](/wiki/Fruit "Fruit"), [beef](/wiki/Beef "Beef"), [tilapia](/wiki/Tilapia "Tilapia"), and other foods. Industries in Tak include [granite](/wiki/Granite "Granite") quarrying and [jewelry](/wiki/Jewelry "Jewelry"). Zinc mining was formerly conducted in [Mae Sot District](/wiki/Mae_Sot_District "Mae Sot District").[[22]](#cite_note-22)

[Handicrafts](/wiki/Handicraft "Handicraft") and [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar") products are also important for trade. The [Bhumibol Dam](/wiki/Bhumibol_Dam "Bhumibol Dam") in the northern part of Tak is its most popular tourist attraction. Tourism, especially [ecotourism](/wiki/Ecotourism "Ecotourism"), in the southern part is seasonal with popular destinations such as the [Thi Lo Su Waterfall](/wiki/Thi_Lo_Su_Waterfall "Thi Lo Su Waterfall"), Thi Lo Le Waterfall, [hiking](/wiki/Hiking "Hiking") and [white water rafting](/wiki/White_water_rafting "White water rafting") in its various forest reserves. Tak is also known for its [Loi Krathong](/wiki/Loi_Krathong "Loi Krathong") festival where *krathong sai* (กระทงสาย) consisting of many krathongs are floated in a long line down the river.[[23]](#cite_note-23) The Loi Krathong festival is held on the [Ping River](/wiki/Ping_River "Ping River") in [Mueang Tak District](/wiki/Mueang_Tak_District "Mueang Tak District") on Loi Krathong night.

### Roads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tak_province&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Roads")]

Tak is a key communication and transportation centre of the north, with three [Asian highways](/wiki/Asian_highway "Asian highway") passing through the province. [AH1](/wiki/AH1 "AH1") enters through the Myanmar-Thai border at [Mae Sot District](/wiki/Mae_Sot_District "Mae Sot District")[[24]](#cite_note-Highways-24) [AH2](/wiki/AH2 "AH2") passes through the province from north to south. Also [AH16](/wiki/AH16 "AH16") terminates at Tak.

### Air

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tak_province&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Air")]

Tak province is served by [Tak Airport](/wiki/Tak_Airport "Tak Airport").
